title: Majority Elements over n/3
description:
### Objective
Return all elements that appear more than ⌊n/3⌋ times. There can be at most two such elements. Use the extended Boyer–Moore algorithm and verify candidates in a final pass.

### Function Contract
Implement `majority_elements_n_over_3(nums: List[int])` in Python.
Do not change input/output semantics expected by the tests.

### Input and Output
- Input `nums`: type `array` (example: `[3,2,3]`).
- Return type: `array`.

### Requirements
- Return the exact expected output for every test case.
- Handle edge cases (empty input, duplicates, boundary values) without raising exceptions.
- Target complexity: time `O(n)`, space `O(1)`.

### Constraints
- 1 ≤ len(nums) ≤ 10^5
- -10^9 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^9
explanation:
Intuition
At threshold n/3 there can be at most two winners. Track two candidates with two counters. On reading a number, increment its candidate's count if matched, else claim an empty slot, else decrement both counts. Then verify counts to confirm winners.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Keep (cand1,c1) and (cand2,c2).
2. Main Algorithm: Single pass to select candidates via cancellation and slot claiming. Second pass to count their occurrences.
3. Return Result: Return those exceeding ⌊n/3⌋.

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n) because the algorithm scans elements a constant
  number of times and performs O(1) work for each update or lookup.
- Space Complexity: O(1) due to the auxiliary structures required to
  store counts, prefix sums, or membership sets while ensuring fast access.

Key Insights
- Bounded number of winners yields constant memory
- Verification ensures correctness without a guarantee

Common Pitfalls
- Returning unverified candidates is incorrect
- Using more than two slots is unnecessary
hints: At most two elements can exceed n/3
Use two candidate slots and counters
Verify candidates in a separate pass
